item.title: Moving to Casselberry, FL? Here's What Orlando Families Need to Know Before Hiring Movers
item.description: Moving to Casselberry starts here. Get local tips on neighborhoods, hidden move-day costs, and how to hire movers you can actually trust in Central Florida.
item.author: Liberty Moves Orlando
item.content:encoded:
Casselberry sits in a sweet spot. You get easy access to Orlando without the downtown price tag. You get Seminole County schools, quiet residential streets, and a short drive to Winter Park, Altamonte Springs, and Lake Mary. Families notice this balance immediately, and it's why so many of them choose [Casselberry](https://libertymovesorlando.com/casselberry-movers) over the more expensive zip codes nearby. But moving here takes more than picking a house and signing a lease. You need to understand the neighborhoods, plan around local traffic patterns, and choose movers who actually know the area. This guide breaks down everything Orlando-area families should know before they hire movers in Casselberry. ## **Why Families Choose Casselberry Over Other Orlando Suburbs** Casselberry offers a few clear advantages. Home prices run lower than Winter Park or Maitland, yet the commute to downtown Orlando stays manageable. Lake Howell anchors several neighborhoods with water access and walking trails. Seminole County schools rank well, and many families relocate specifically for that reason. The city also sits at a major crossroads. Highway 17-92 and SR-436 intersect here, which means quick trips to Winter Park, Altamonte Springs, or the I-4 corridor. Commuters appreciate this. Parents appreciate the proximity to good schools without the price tag attached to them. ## **What Makes Moving Logistics in Casselberry Different** Casselberry presents a few specific challenges that families should plan around before move day. **Older neighborhoods mean narrower streets.** Many residential areas near Lake Howell and along 17-92 were built decades ago. Driveways run shorter, and some streets don't accommodate a large moving truck comfortably. A crew unfamiliar with the area might struggle with positioning. **Traffic on 17-92 backs up during peak hours.** Morning and evening commutes slow significantly along this corridor. Movers who know Casselberry plan around these windows instead of getting stuck in them. **Apartment and condo complexes vary widely.** Some buildings near the SR-436 corridor have limited parking and no formal loading zone. Others require advance notice before a moving truck can access the property. Confirm these details with your building before booking your move date. **Storms roll through quickly in summer.** Florida's daily thunderstorm pattern hits Casselberry the same way it hits the rest of Central Florida. A morning start protects your move from afternoon downpours and keeps your belongings dry. ## **How Much Does It Cost to Hire Movers in Casselberry?** Pricing depends on a few factors: home size, distance, crew size, and whether you need packing help. For families researching [moving companies near me](https://libertymovesorlando.com/local-movers) in the Casselberry area, here's a general framework. A studio or one-bedroom apartment typically takes a two-person crew two to three hours. A three-bedroom house usually requires a four-person crew and runs five to seven hours. Long-distance moves out of Casselberry price differently, based on weight and mileage rather than hourly rates. The [Orlando moving cost guide](https://libertymovesorlando.com/orlando-moving-cost) breaks this down further, with real benchmarks by home size so families can budget accurately before they request quotes. ## **Neighborhoods Families Should Know Before They Move** **Lake Howell area.** This neighborhood draws families who want water access and a quieter residential feel. Streets here are narrower, and movers need to plan truck positioning carefully. **Near 17-92.** This corridor mixes apartments, townhomes, and older single-family homes. Traffic moves slower during commute hours, so an early start avoids delays. **SR-436 corridor.** Several apartment and condo complexes sit along this stretch. Parking and loading-zone access vary by building, so confirm logistics with your property manager before your move date. **Near Seminole Plaza.** This area combines shopping convenience with residential streets close to schools. Families relocating with kids often gravitate here for the walkability. ## **What to Ask Before You Hire Movers in Casselberry** A few questions separate a reliable mover from a risky one. Ask whether the company is licensed through FDACS, which requires an IM number for any company moving belongings within Florida. Ask whether the estimate is binding or subject to change. Ask how the company handles narrow streets or limited parking — Casselberry has plenty of both, and an experienced crew will already know how to navigate them. A trustworthy [local mover in Orlando](https://libertymovesorlando.com/local-movers) answers these questions directly, without hesitation or vague reassurances. ## **Why Local Experience Matters More Than National Brand Recognition** National movers know general best practices. Local movers know Casselberry specifically — which streets get tight near Lake Howell, which apartment complexes require advance notice, and which routes avoid 17-92 traffic during the worst hours of the day. This local knowledge saves time on moving day. It also reduces the risk of damage, since experienced crews already understand how to navigate narrow driveways and older home layouts common throughout the city. Families relocating with kids benefit even more from this familiarity. A crew that moves efficiently finishes faster, which means less disruption to nap schedules, less time outside in Florida heat, and fewer hours spent waiting around a half-packed house. ## **Planning Your Move Timeline** Start the process about four weeks before your move date. Confirm your move-out and move-in dates with both property managers or closing agents. Request quotes from a few [Orlando moving companies](https://libertymovesorlando.com/local-movers) and compare them line by line, not just by price. Two weeks out, confirm your crew size, arrival window, and any building-specific requirements like elevator access or loading permits. One week out, finish packing and prepare an essentials bag with documents, chargers, and anything you'll need immediately at the new house. If your relocation extends beyond Central Florida, Liberty Moves Orlando also handles [long-distance moves](https://libertymovesorlando.com/long-distance-moving) out of the Casselberry area, with the same attention to planning and communication. ## **The Bottom Line** Casselberry gives families a genuine balance — good schools, manageable commutes, and a quieter pace than downtown Orlando offers. Moving here smoothly takes a little planning: understand the neighborhood you're moving into, account for narrow streets and 17-92 traffic, and confirm building logistics before your move date arrives. Liberty Moves Orlando is based right here in [Casselberry](https://libertymovesorlando.com/casselberry-movers). We know the streets, the apartment complexes, and the traffic patterns better than movers coming in from outside the area. We're fully licensed (USDOT 3455436, FLDACS IM3347), family-owned, and built specifically to serve Central Florida families. Request a free quote at [libertymovesorlando.com/contact-us](https://libertymovesorlando.com/contact-us) or call **(407) 641-2887** to start planning your move.
## **Frequently Asked Questions** ### **How much do movers cost in Casselberry, FL?** Pricing depends on home size and crew requirements. A one-bedroom apartment typically runs two to three hours with a two-person crew, while a three-bedroom home usually takes five to seven hours with a four-person crew. Long-distance moves price by weight and mileage instead. ### **What neighborhoods in Casselberry have the most moving logistics challenges?** Areas near Lake Howell have narrower streets and shorter driveways, which require careful truck positioning. The SR-436 corridor includes several apartment complexes with limited parking, so confirming access with your property manager beforehand helps avoid delays. ### **Do I need to book movers in advance for a Casselberry move?** Booking three to four weeks ahead gives you the best availability, particularly for weekend dates. If your building requires a Certificate of Insurance or elevator reservation, earlier booking gives your mover time to handle that paperwork. ### **Are Liberty Moves Orlando's movers familiar with Casselberry specifically?** Yes. Liberty Moves Orlando is headquartered in Casselberry, and our crews handle moves throughout the area regularly. We know the older neighborhoods near Lake Howell, the traffic patterns on 17-92, and the apartment logistics along SR-436. ### **What's the best time of day to schedule a move in Casselberry during summer?** Morning starts work best. Florida's typical afternoon thunderstorms develop by early-to-mid afternoon throughout summer, so completing your move before that window keeps your belongings dry and your crew working efficiently.

item.title: Apartment Moving in Orlando: What Nobody Tells You Before Move-In Day
item.description: The real insider guide to apartment moving in Orlando — elevators, COIs, HOA windows, Florida heat, and what actually goes wrong before move-in day.
item.author: Liberty Moves Orlando
item.content:encoded: Every apartment move looks simple on paper. You've got a lease, a date, and a truck. What could go wrong? Plenty — and most of it isn't obvious until you're standing in a loading zone at 8 a.m. with a crew, a full truck, and a property manager telling you the elevator wasn't reserved for today. Apartment moving in Orlando has its own specific set of challenges that nobody warns you about in advance. Not the leasing office. Not the moving company listing on Google. Not even friends who've moved here before — because the rules vary building by building, and the margin for error on move-in day is tighter than most people expect. Here's what actually happens, and how to be the person who has it handled before the truck pulls up. ## **The Elevator Reservation Nobody Reminded You to Make** This is the single most common reason Orlando apartment moves go sideways. Most mid-rise and high-rise buildings across the metro — in downtown Orlando, Baldwin Park, Lake Nona, Altamonte Springs, and Winter Park — require tenants to reserve the service elevator in advance. Not the passenger elevator. The service elevator, which is often in a separate location, has a padded interior, and runs on a scheduled window that other residents aren't supposed to interfere with. Miss that window and your options are a stairwell move (slower, harder, more damage risk) or waiting until another slot opens — which may not be the same day. Most buildings require the reservation 1–2 weeks in advance. Some require a refundable deposit to hold it. Some only offer 2-hour windows on weekends, which means if your crew runs 20 minutes behind, you're already behind the clock. The fix is simple: call the property management office the day you sign your lease — not the week of your move. Get the elevator policy in writing. Pass it to your [apartment movers in Orlando](https://libertymovesorlando.com/apartment-movers-orlando) when you book so they can plan around the window. ## **The COI Requirement Your New Building Forgot to Mention** Certificate of Insurance requirements have become standard at most managed apartment complexes in Central Florida — particularly in newer developments in Lake Nona, Dr. Phillips, and along the I-4 corridor. A COI names the building or property management company as an additional insured party. It's their protection, not yours. But here's the catch: they usually tell you about it in the move-in welcome packet — which you often receive 3–5 days before your move date, when some moving companies need 5–10 business days to process the request. Liberty Moves Orlando provides building-specific COIs at no charge. If you're booking [local movers](https://libertymovesorlando.com/local-movers) and your building requires one, ask for it the moment you book — not the day before. ## **HOA Moving Windows Are Real and They Will Lock You Out** Gated communities and HOA-managed apartment complexes in Windermere, Baldwin Park, Celebration, and Winter Park operate on their own timeline. A significant number of them restrict moving activity to specific hours — usually 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. on weekdays, with limited or no weekend availability. If you've booked a Saturday morning move and your HOA only allows weekday moves, you're going to find out about it when the gate doesn't open for the truck. Call your HOA or property management company before you finalize your moving date. Ask specifically: are there designated moving hours? Do we need to register the moving company or truck in advance? Is there a loading zone assignment process? Get the answers before they become day-of emergencies. ## **Florida Heat Isn't Just Uncomfortable — It's a Logistics Problem** **An 8 a.m. start in July isn't early. It's necessary.** Orlando temperatures hit the mid-90s by late morning throughout summer, and the heat index inside a moving truck or on an asphalt parking lot can climb well past that. For your crew, that means slower pace and more rest breaks as the day goes on. For your belongings, it means candles melt, electronics overheat, and anything stored in the truck while crews carry items inside is sitting in a 120-degree box. For a stress-free move in Orlando, start as early as your building allows. Schedule your move for completion before noon if possible. And for anything heat-sensitive — vinyl records, artwork, certain cosmetics and medications — keep those with you in an air-conditioned vehicle, not on the truck. If you're moving to or from Fern Park, Casselberry, or the SR-436 corridor where buildings tend to be older with fewer shaded loading areas, a morning start isn't a preference — it's a practical strategy. ## **The Loading Zone That Isn't Actually Available** Apartment complexes list a "loading zone" on the move-in instructions. What they don't always mention is that it's shared by every resident, holds one truck comfortably, and has no reservation system. Show up at 9 a.m. on a Saturday in a building where three other people are also moving and you may be waiting 45 minutes just for access to the loading area. For communities in [Winter Park](https://libertymovesorlando.com/winter-park-orlando) — especially buildings near Park Avenue, Full Sail, or UCF — this is a real consideration. A moving crew that knows how to work around it makes a measurable difference in whether your four-hour move stays four hours. ## **What You Packed "Last Minute" Will Cost You the Most Time** [Professional movers](https://libertymovesorlando.com/residential-moving) see this on almost every apartment job: the boxes that weren't ready. The coffee maker still on the counter. The bathroom cabinet that hasn't been touched. The closet shelf with things you were "going to deal with later." Each of those pockets adds 20–45 minutes to the job — and in an apartment building where you have a hard elevator window or a tight parking situation, that time compounds fast. The solution isn't a faster crew. It's packing everything — everything — before the truck arrives. If that's genuinely not possible, [professional packing services](https://libertymovesorlando.com/packing-services) are almost always faster and cheaper than extending a move by 2 hours because half the apartment wasn't ready. ## **The Stuff That Goes on the Truck Last Should Have Come Off First** This is a packing logic mistake that happens constantly on apartment moves and is completely avoidable. The items you'll need first in your new apartment — bed frame hardware, the box with your shower curtain and toilet paper, your coffee setup, the TV remote — should be the last things loaded onto the truck so they're the first things off. Instead, most people pack them first, bury them at the back of the truck, and spend the first evening digging through 40 boxes to find a phone charger. Before the truck gets loaded, set aside an "open first" box and load it last. Label it clearly. Tell your crew it goes straight to the designated room and comes off the truck before anything else. ## **Don't Book the Truck Before You Know the Parking Situation** Moving trucks need real space — not just a spot in a parking lot, but enough clearance to maneuver, get the ramp down, and park close enough to the building entrance that carries don't turn into marathons. Before move day, find out: where is the designated moving truck area? Is it on the street or in the lot? Is there a height clearance for the garage? Does the truck need to be registered with property management? For last-minute situations where logistics didn't fall into place until the week of the move, Liberty Moves Orlando's [same-day moving service](https://libertymovesorlando.com/last-minute-movers-orlando) is built for exactly that — coordinating fast, with the building realities already accounted for. ## **The Neighborhoods Where Apartment Moves Get Complicated Fastest** - **Downtown Orlando and Thornton Park:** Narrow streets, metered parking, limited loading zones, and buildings with strict elevator policies. Weekday moves are often smoother than weekends here. - **Winter Park:** Older buildings near Park Avenue have tight alleyways and brick streets that restrict truck access. Newer high-rises near I-4 have their own elevator and COI policies. Moving companies serving this area need to know the building before they show up. - **Fern Park and Casselberry:** Older apartment complexes along 17-92 and SR-436 often have limited parking, no designated loading zones, and longer carries from parking to unit. A crew that knows this corridor — which Liberty Moves Orlando does, operating out of Casselberry — plans the truck position and carry routes before a single box comes off. - **Lake Nona:** Newer communities with strict HOA rules, gated access requirements, and often very specific move-in protocols. Start your coordination with the HOA 2–3 weeks out. ## **The Bottom Line** Apartment moving in Orlando isn't complicated — but it's specific. The buildings have rules. The weather has opinions. The loading zones have politics. And move-in day has no patience for surprises. Do the coordination 2–3 weeks out: elevator reservation, COI, HOA moving window, parking logistics. Pack completely before the crew arrives. Start early. And hire movers who've worked in your building's neighborhood enough times to know what to expect before they get there. Liberty Moves Orlando handles apartment moves across all of Central Florida — fully licensed (USDOT 3455436, FLDACS IM3347), COI-ready, and familiar with the loading zones, HOA rules, and elevator policies across the metro. Get a free quote at [libertymovesorlando.com/contact-us](https://libertymovesorlando.com/contact-us) or call **(407) 641-2887** — and mention your building so we can flag anything specific before move day. ## **Frequently Asked Questions** ### **Q: How far in advance should I book apartment movers in Orlando?** **A:** Book at least 3–4 weeks out for weekend and end-of-month dates, which fill fastest. If your building requires a COI, booking early gives your mover time to process it and send it to your property manager before move day. ### **Q: What is a COI and why does my apartment building need one?** **A:** A Certificate of Insurance is a document your moving company provides that names the building or property management company as an additional insured party. It protects the building if damage occurs during your move. Most managed properties in Central Florida require it — Liberty Moves Orlando provides these at no charge. ### **Q: Do you handle apartment moves in Fern Park and Casselberry?** **A:** Yes. Liberty Moves Orlando is based in Casselberry and regularly handles apartment and condo moves along the SR-436 and 17-92 corridor, including Fern Park. We know the parking situations, building layouts, and carry distances in that area well. ### Q: **What's the best time of day to start an apartment move in Orlando?** **A:** In summer months (May–September), a 7:30–8 a.m. start is ideal — it gets the heavy work done before the heat peaks and before afternoon thunderstorms develop. For buildings with restricted elevator windows, your start time may be determined by the reservation, but early is almost always better in Florida. ### **Q: Can you provide a COI for my apartment building the same week?** **A:** In most cases yes — Liberty Moves Orlando processes COI requests quickly. Contact us with your property manager's requirements as soon as you have them, and we'll get it sent over before your move date.

item.title: How to Choose a Moving Company
item.content:encoded:
## The 7 questions every honest mover answers fast
1. **"What’s your USDOT number and FLDACS IM#?"** Any legitimate mover has both for Florida intrastate moves. Verify at safer.fmcsa.dot.gov and [FDACS Business Search](https://csapp.fdacs.gov/cspublicapp/businesssearch/businesssearch.aspx). 2. **"Can you send me a written estimate?"** "Yes, same day" is the only right answer. 3. **"What’s included in the hourly rate?"** Should include truck, crew, pads, shrink wrap, dollies, tape, mileage, fuel. Anything else separate is a red flag. 4. **"What coverage options do you offer?"** Released Value (free, 60¢/lb), Full Value Protection, and optional third-party insurance. See our [insurance guide](https://libertymovesorlando.com/blog/moving-insurance-explained). 5. **"Do you subcontract?"** Best answer: "No." Subcontractors mean your stuff gets handled by people the company you hired doesn’t control. 6. **"What’s your damage claims process?"** Clear timeline (usually 30 days), clear contact, straightforward. Vague answers = slow payouts. 7. **"Can I see recent reviews?"** Google Business Profile, not just testimonials on their own site. 100+ reviews with 4.5+ stars.
## 6 red flags that mean walk away
- **Cash-only payment.** Real businesses take cards and checks. Cash-only often means unlicensed. - **Large deposit required.** Local moves under $2,000 shouldn’t need a deposit. Long-distance maybe 10% refundable 48 hours out. Anything else is a scam pattern. - **Unmarked truck or rental.** Legitimate movers drive their own branded equipment. - **No written estimate.** Verbal quotes become “final price” arguments at destination, holding your stuff hostage. - **Surprise fees on move day.** Run from any mover adding charges after the truck is loaded. - **Huge price gap vs competitors.** 40% below the market usually means they’ll find ways to make up the gap — usually by damaging your stuff.
## Where to find reputable Orlando movers
- **Google Business Profile** — look for 100+ reviews, 4.5+ stars, active responses to reviews - **BBB** — A+ rating + no unresolved complaints - **FMCSA database** — safer.fmcsa.dot.gov shows insurance + authority status - **FDACS** — [csapp.fdacs.gov](https://csapp.fdacs.gov/cspublicapp/businesssearch/businesssearch.aspx) for Florida intrastate license verification - **AMSA/Pro Mover program** — voluntary trade association with standards
## The honest pitch
We’re Liberty Moves Orlando. USDOT 3455436. FLDACS IM3347. 300+ Google reviews. Licensed, insured, transparent. If you’re comparing us to other Orlando movers — ask them the 7 questions above. Let them earn your business. If we don’t, hire someone else. That’s the right way to make this decision.
[Get my Liberty Moves quote](https://libertymovesorlando.com/contact-us)[Call (407) 641-2887](tel:+14076412887)

item.title: Best Orlando Neighborhoods for Families
item.content:encoded:
## 1. Oviedo (Seminole County)
**Median home: $475k · Avg school rating: 9/10 · Commute to downtown: 30 min**
Consistently rated one of Florida’s best family cities. Seminole County schools are top-tier statewide. Quiet, family-oriented, low crime. Drawback: commute to downtown Orlando is slow during rush hour.
## 2. Windermere (Orange County)
**Median home: $950k · Avg school rating: 9/10 · Commute: 25 min**
Luxury lakefront neighborhood with excellent schools (Windermere HS top-rated). Tiger Woods lives here. Gated, safe, highly desirable. Price tag matches — among Central Florida’s most expensive.
## 3. Lake Mary (Seminole County)
**Median home: $525k · Avg school rating: 9/10 · Commute: 25 min**
Executive homes, corporate relocation hub, Heathrow & Timacuan neighborhoods prized. Great schools, easy I-4 access. Balanced mix of nature and convenience.
## 4. Winter Park (Orange County)
**Median home: $750k · Avg school rating: 8/10 · Commute: 10 min**
Historic, walkable, close to downtown. Rollins College, Park Avenue shopping, brick streets. Best if you want urban amenities + family feel. Schools good, not the absolute top.
## 5. Winter Springs (Seminole County)
**Median home: $425k · Avg school rating: 8/10 · Commute: 35 min**
Tuscawilla golf community. Good schools, quiet, lots of families. More affordable than Oviedo or Lake Mary. Commute to downtown is the main tradeoff.
## 6. Lake Nona (Orange County)
**Median home: $625k · Avg school rating: 8/10 · Commute: 25 min**
Master-planned community with Medical City, USTA National Campus, tech corridor. Everything is new. Lots of young families. Near Orlando airport — good for frequent travelers.
## 7. Winter Garden (Orange County)
**Median home: $500k · Avg school rating: 8/10 · Commute: 30 min**
Horizon West is the boom — brand-new master-planned communities with new schools. Walkable downtown Winter Garden is charming. Fast-growing but traffic is catching up.
## 8. Baldwin Park (Orange County)
**Median home: $725k · Avg school rating: 8/10 · Commute: 5 min**
New-urbanism neighborhood right next to downtown. Walkable, Lake Baldwin views, townhomes and single-family. Great if you work downtown and want a tight-knit community feel.
## 9. Apopka (Orange County)
**Median home: $400k · Avg school rating: 7/10 · Commute: 30 min**
Best value on this list. Bigger homes and yards for less money. Schools are decent (not top-tier). Great for families prioritizing space and budget over school rankings.
## 10. Celebration (Osceola County)
**Median home: $650k · Avg school rating: 8/10 · Commute: 30 min**
Disney-built town with a distinctive look — clean, walkable, planned. Town center, lakes, manicured. Great K-8 school on-site. Feels different from typical Florida suburbs.
## How to choose for your family
- **Prioritize schools:** Oviedo, Windermere, Lake Mary - **Prioritize budget:** Apopka, Winter Springs - **Prioritize downtown access:** Baldwin Park, Winter Park - **Prioritize "new":** Lake Nona, Winter Garden Horizon West - **Prioritize lakefront:** Windermere, Lake Mary
[Get a quote for your neighborhood](https://libertymovesorlando.com/contact-us)[Moving with kids guide →](https://libertymovesorlando.com/blog/moving-to-orlando-with-kids)

item.title: Moving to Orlando with Kids
item.content:encoded:
## Pick the school district first, the house second
In Orlando, school district determines everything — home price, your weekly routine, your kid’s friends. The four big districts families relocate into:
- **Seminole County Public Schools** — consistently top-rated statewide. Oviedo, Lake Mary, Winter Springs, Sanford. - **Orange County Public Schools** — larger district with huge variance. Dr. Phillips, Windermere, Winter Park, Lake Nona all excellent. - **Osceola County** — fast-growing, newer schools. Celebration K–8, Harmony Community School, parts of St. Cloud. - **Lake County** — smaller, rural feel. Mount Dora, Eustis, Montverde Academy (private).
## Transfer your kid’s school records
1. Request official records from current school 60 days out (transcripts, immunizations, IEP/504 plans if applicable). 2. Contact your new school directly — principal’s office, not just the district website. 3. Florida requires up-to-date immunizations (blue form 680) and physical exam (yellow form 3040) — bookmark the FL DOH websites. 4. Submit enrollment forms to new school 30+ days ahead. 5. Request a school tour before the first day if possible — reduces first-day anxiety.
## Best Orlando neighborhoods for families
- **Oviedo** — top-rated Seminole schools, family-oriented communities, walking trails. - **Lake Mary** — executive homes, Timacuan/Heathrow neighborhoods, short commute to downtown. - **Winter Springs** — Tuscawilla golf community, high-rated schools, lots of kids. - **Windermere** — luxury schools, safe gated communities, lakefront homes. - **Winter Garden** — Horizon West is booming, brand-new schools, walkable downtown. - **Lake Nona** — master-planned, new schools, Medical City jobs nearby.
## Helping kids adjust
- **Involve them.** Let them pick their room, their paint color, their room layout. - **Find their "thing" fast.** Sign up for sports/arts/clubs week 1 — friendships form in activities, not classrooms. - **Keep one ritual from the old city.** Friday pizza, Sunday pancakes — whatever was a tradition. Continuity matters. - **Talk to teachers.** Let them know the kid is new. Good teachers will check in. - **Give it a semester.** Real adjustment takes 3–4 months. Don’t panic in month 1.
## Kid-friendly Orlando (beyond theme parks)
- Orlando Science Center, Crayola Experience, Orlando Museum of Art - Lake Eola Park (swan boats, downtown playground, farmers market Sunday) - Wekiwa Springs State Park (kayaking, swimming, 30 min from downtown) - Blue Spring State Park (manatees November–March) - Kennedy Space Center (1 hour east) - Daytona Beach & Cocoa Beach (under 90 min either direction)
[Quote my family move](https://libertymovesorlando.com/contact-us)[Neighborhood comparison →](https://libertymovesorlando.com/blog/best-orlando-neighborhoods-for-families)

item.title: Moving During Hurricane Season Florida
item.content:encoded:
## When hurricane risk actually matters
Florida hurricane season officially runs June 1 to November 30. But the real danger window is narrower — **mid-August through October**. Early season (June, July) storms happen but are usually disorganized. Late season (November) storms are rare and weak. If you can pick your dates, avoid September in particular.
## Monitoring before your move
- **5 days out:** Check NOAA’s National Hurricane Center (nhc.noaa.gov) and the Central Florida weather forecast. - **3 days out:** If anything is in the Gulf or Atlantic that could impact Florida, call your mover to discuss. - **48 hours out:** Decision time. If a named storm is within 72 hours of landfall anywhere near Florida, reschedule. - **24 hours out:** Too late to start a move if a storm is bearing down. Shelter-in-place instead.
## What to do if you must move during an active threat
Sometimes the move date can’t shift — closing day, lease expiration, PCS order. In that case:
- Start earlier in the day — finish before weather worsens - Skip non-essentials — take the essentials, leave decor for later - Pack everything that could be damaged by water in plastic first - Secure the truck at destination; don’t leave it loaded overnight - Get everything inside and out of yards before any wind arrives
## Rescheduling rules at most movers
Reputable Florida movers don’t charge for weather-forced reschedules when a state or local emergency is declared. Liberty Moves Orlando specifically: free rescheduling when a named storm is within 72 hours of projected Florida landfall. No fees, no fights.
## What to pack first (for hurricane-zone moves)
If there’s any hurricane risk during your move window, prioritize packing items that can’t be replaced:
- Important documents (passports, birth certs, tax records) — waterproof bag, in your car - Irreplaceable photos, heirlooms, art — first in the truck or first in your car - Medications for 7 days — in a labeled bag, kept with you - Electronics — wrapped in plastic before packed
## Insurance considerations
Most mover insurance (Released Value + Full Value Protection) does NOT cover "acts of God" — including hurricanes. If your truck is damaged by a storm in transit, you’re often on your own. Read more in our [moving insurance guide](https://libertymovesorlando.com/blog/moving-insurance-explained).
[Book a safe move date](https://libertymovesorlando.com/contact-us)[Call (407) 641-2887](tel:+14076412887)

item.title: What Movers Wont Move
item.content:encoded:
## Hazardous materials
Federal regulations prohibit any licensed mover from carrying hazmat. Fire, explosion, and poisoning risk in a closed truck.
- Propane tanks (grills, camping) - Gasoline (lawn mower, generator fuel) - Paint (oil-based is hazmat, water-based technically allowed but often refused) - Pool chemicals (chlorine, acids) - Lighter fluid, charcoal lighter - Aerosols (spray paint, bug spray, hair spray in large quantity) - Cleaning supplies with bleach or ammonia - Batteries (especially car + boat batteries)
**What to do:** Use them up or dispose at Orange County’s hazmat drop-off (one location on Young Pine Rd). Don’t try to sneak them onto the truck.
## Firearms and ammunition
Licensed movers won’t transport firearms or ammunition. Liability, insurance, and legal complications.
**What to do:** Transport yourself in your own vehicle. Check Florida + destination state laws if moving interstate. FFL transfer might be required for long-distance.
## Perishable food
Fridges and freezers get unplugged for the move. Frozen food thaws, refrigerated food spoils in 70°+ Florida trucks. Movers won’t take the liability.
**What to do:** Eat it down in the 2 weeks before the move. Donate unopened pantry items. Empty + defrost fridge 24 hours before move day.
## Plants (especially interstate)
USDA restricts plants crossing state lines due to invasive species and pest risk. Some states (California, Arizona) prohibit almost all plants from Florida. Local moves within Florida are fine but plants often don’t survive the truck anyway.
**What to do:** Gift to neighbors, sell on Facebook Marketplace, or transport in your car for short moves. Accept that some plants won’t survive.
## Cash, jewelry, important documents
Movers won’t take liability for items above $100/lb without declaration, and won’t touch cash at all.
**What to do:** Pack in your personal vehicle. Passports, tax records, jewelry, cash, family heirlooms — always with you, never on the truck.
## Other common refusals
- Animals (pets travel with you) - Opened or partially used food containers - Perishables from gardens (produce from your garden) - Items with nostalgic value above $100/lb that weren’t declared - Some movers decline mattresses in very poor condition
## Pro tip: The "Essentials Box"
Pack a single box or bag that stays with you, NOT on the truck:
- Passports, IDs, important documents - Medications (prescriptions + OTC) - Cash, checkbook, jewelry - 2 days of clothes - Phone chargers + laptop - Pet supplies (day-of) - Toiletries, snacks, water
[Get a quote (with non-allowables list)](https://libertymovesorlando.com/contact-us)[Call (407) 641-2887](tel:+14076412887)

item.title: Office Relocation Planning Guide
item.content:encoded:
## 90 days out — Strategy
- Finalize new lease and move-in date - Lock budget (typically $1–$2/sqft for commercial moves in Orlando) - Get 3 written quotes from commercial movers - Name an internal move lead (not the CEO, not the office manager juggling everything — dedicated person) - Communicate move to staff — set expectations, not details yet
## 60 days out — IT & Infrastructure
- Order internet / fiber at new office — this is often the 60-day bottleneck - Plan server rack decommission and reinstall - Schedule IT vendor walkthroughs at both locations - Order new furniture if needed — 8+ week lead times common - Begin space planning — seating charts, meeting room assignments
## 30 days out — Logistics
- Book your moving company (Liberty Moves Orlando handles commercial weekends regularly) - Submit COI requests to both landlords - Schedule elevator reservations and loading dock access - Order moving boxes, labels, tape (or get from mover at cost) - Update business address: bank, insurance, licenses, Secretary of State, Google Business Profile - Update vendor notifications: UPS, FedEx, suppliers, mail forwarding
## 2 weeks out — Execution prep
- Start staff self-packing of desks (personal items, books, files) - Final walkthrough with mover — confirm access, parking, scope - Label everything with destination room/desk - IT: begin backup + decommission plan - Cancel/reroute utility and service accounts
## Move weekend — Execution
- **Friday 5pm:** Staff out. Movers start. IT pulls backup drives. - **Saturday:** Pack + load old office. Furniture disassembly. Truck to new office. Install at new location. - **Sunday:** IT reconnects — network, servers, phone, printers. Test everything. Fix missing items. - **Monday 8am:** Staff arrive. Orientation. Business runs.
## Week 1 at new location
- Walk every desk, fix issues, order missing items - Update directory, reception info, phone extensions - File any damage claims within 30 days - Host a team lunch — low-friction morale boost for the chaos
## Common mistakes to avoid
- **Underestimating IT.** Network and phone are 70% of office-move failures. - **Not getting COIs to landlords early.** Last-minute COIs delay elevator access. - **Letting staff pack their own PCs.** Standardize — IT handles all tech. - **Moving into a half-finished space.** If the new office isn’t ready, delay. - **Skipping the professional mover.** Commercial moves are not a DIY job.
[See our commercial moving service](https://libertymovesorlando.com/commercial-moving)[Quote my office move](https://libertymovesorlando.com/contact-us)

item.title: Furniture Disassembly Guide
item.content:encoded:
## Tools you’ll actually need
- Phillips + flathead screwdrivers - Allen key / hex key set (IKEA, modern furniture) - Adjustable wrench (bed frames, tables) - Ziplock bags (quart size for hardware) - Masking tape + Sharpie (labels) - Your phone camera
## The labeling system that saves you hours
1. Before you touch a tool: take photos of the furniture from 4 angles 2. Remove hardware into a ziplock bag 3. Write on the bag in Sharpie: *"BED FRAME — QUEEN — LEFT SIDE RAIL"* 4. Tape the bag directly to the furniture piece (on the underside or back) 5. If the furniture is coming apart into multiple pieces, label each piece with masking tape
## Bed frames
Standard process: remove mattress and box spring. Remove side rails (usually 4 bolts). Remove headboard and footboard from rails. Keep hardware bag taped to one rail. Reassemble is the reverse — takes ~30 min per bed.
## Dining tables
Most tables: flip table upside down on a blanket to protect finish. Unscrew legs (usually 4 bolts each). Keep all hardware in one labeled bag taped to the underside of the table. Reassemble: 15 min.
## Dressers + bookcases
Generally don’t need to disassemble unless they’re too wide to fit through doorways. Empty all drawers and pack contents separately. Remove drawers for the move (lighter + easier to handle). Take photos first of any mirror attachments for reassembly.
## Cribs
Cribs MUST be disassembled — they’re too fragile to move assembled. Save all hardware carefully. Keep the instruction manual if you still have it. If you don’t, search the manufacturer website for reassembly steps. For safety, inspect all hardware before putting baby back in.
## IKEA + modern furniture
Keep original Allen keys taped to the item. Label every panel before removing. Take photos of every dowel hole before disassembling. IKEA furniture typically doesn’t love being disassembled/reassembled — if you can move it assembled, do.
## Desks + home office
Remove monitor arms, cable management, drawer contents. Photograph cable setups before disconnecting. Most desks disassemble like tables.
## When we handle it for you
Liberty Moves Orlando includes basic furniture disassembly and reassembly at no extra charge — beds, tables, cribs, standard pieces. We bring our own tools, we keep hardware labeled, and we handle reassembly at your destination. Complex items (custom cabinets, pool tables, entertainment centers) may be quoted separately.
[Quote my move (disassembly included)](https://libertymovesorlando.com/contact-us)[Packing services →](https://libertymovesorlando.com/packing-services)

item.title: Storage Unit Sizes Guide
item.content:encoded:
## Orlando storage pricing at a glance
Orlando storage ranges from $50/month for tiny lockers to $400+/month for large climate-controlled garage units. **Climate control adds $30–$60/month** vs non-climate — worth it in Florida’s humidity.
## 5×5 — The closet
25 sq ft. Fits: 10–20 boxes, 1 mattress, a bike, small items. **$50–$90/month** in Orlando.
*Best for:* Seasonal gear (holiday decor, ski gear before you sold it), college-student storage between semesters, business document archives.
## 5×10 — The walk-in
50 sq ft. Fits: studio apartment worth of stuff. Mattress, small couch, bike, 30+ boxes. **$90–$130/month**.
*Best for:* Studio apartment clear-out, small home office, short-term move gap.
## 10×10 — One bedroom
100 sq ft. Fits: full 1-bedroom apartment — queen bed, sofa, dining set, boxes. **$130–$180/month**.
*Best for:* 1-bed apartment dwellers, roommate splits, mid-size business storage.
## 10×15 — Two bedroom
150 sq ft. Fits: 2-bedroom apartment or small house contents. King bed + queen bed + living room + kitchen boxes. **$180–$240/month**.
*Best for:* Growing families, 2-bed apartment moves, seasonal business inventory.
## 10×20 — Two to three bedroom house
200 sq ft. Fits: 2–3 bedroom home contents. Multiple beds, full living room, full dining, kitchen, outdoor. **$240–$320/month**.
*Best for:* Downsizing, closing gap between selling and buying, commercial seasonal inventory.
## 10×25 or 10×30 — Full house
250–300 sq ft. Fits: 3–4+ bedroom home contents. Think garage-sized. **$300–$420/month**.
*Best for:* Full-home storage, estate transitions, vehicle storage + household.
## Climate control — yes or no?
In Florida: **yes**, for almost everything you care about. Our heat + humidity destroys unprotected:
- Wood furniture (warps, cracks) - Electronics (corrosion) - Leather (mildew) - Paper + photos (yellowing, sticking) - Artwork
Non-climate is OK for: outdoor tools, lawn equipment, metal furniture you don’t care about.
## How to pick the right size
- **Smaller than you think.** Most people overbuy storage — 25–40% oversize. - **Stack vertically.** 10ft ceilings double your effective space. - **Leave a walking path.** If you need to access stuff, don’t pack to the doors. - **Month-to-month contracts.** Never sign annual storage contracts — your needs change.
[Our storage + moving service](https://libertymovesorlando.com/storage-moving-orlando)[Call (407) 641-2887](tel:+14076412887)

item.title: The Real Cost of Hiring the Wrong Movers in Orlando (And How to Avoid It)
item.description: Moving company hidden fees orlando can cost you hundreds extra. Learn what shady movers charge for — and how Liberty Moves Orlando keeps it honest.
item.author: Liberty Moves Orlando
item.content:encoded:
Picture this. You find a moving company with a price that looks great, book them two weeks out, and feel good about the decision. Then moving day arrives — and the truck is an hour late, two guys show up instead of four, and by the time it's over, your dresser has a gouge down the side and one box is missing entirely.

The quote was $350. The final bill is $620. And the dresser replacement? Another $400 out of pocket.

This isn't a rare horror story. It's one of the most common experiences people have when they choose Orlando movers based on price alone — and it costs Central Florida residents far more than they ever expect.

Why Hiring the Wrong Movers Can Cost More Than You Think

The upfront quote is only one part of the financial picture.

When a moving company cuts corners on training, equipment, staffing, or insurance, the true cost usually shows up after the truck drives away. Damaged furniture that isn't covered under a flimsy released-value policy. A delayed move-in because the crew ran out of time and had to reschedule. Extra nights in a hotel because your new place wasn't ready when the movers bailed mid-job.

Hidden fees are another layer. Stair charges, long-carry fees, fuel surcharges, and packing material costs are routinely excluded from low initial estimates — then added to the final invoice once your belongings are already loaded. At that point, you've got very little room to negotiate.

Before you book, it's worth getting a realistic sense of what a properly staffed, fully equipped Orlando move actually costs. The Orlando moving cost guide breaks down local pricing by home size and crew configuration so you know what a legitimate quote looks like before any conversation starts.

Common Red Flags When Evaluating Moving Companies in Orlando

Most bad moving experiences are avoidable. The signals are usually there before you sign anything.

Unusually low estimates. If one quote is dramatically cheaper than every other company you've spoken with, it's worth asking why. Low quotes often exclude fees that appear later — or reflect a company that's underinsured and understaffed.

No license or insurance on file. Every legitimate local moving company in Orlando operating within Florida must hold an FLDACS IM number. Interstate movers need a USDOT number on top of that. Both are verifiable in minutes through official government databases. Liberty Moves Orlando carries FLDACS IM3347 and USDOT 3455436 — publicly verifiable, always current.

No written contract. A legitimate mover provides a written estimate and a Bill of Lading before anything gets loaded. "We'll sort it out day-of" is not a business practice — it's a setup.

Vague or patterned reviews. One bad review is noise. A pattern of complaints about surprise charges, no-shows, or damaged items across Google, BBB, and Yelp is a warning. Look at how the company responds to negative reviews just as much as the reviews themselves.

Large upfront cash deposits. Standard deposits from licensed movers are modest and paid by card. A company demanding significant cash before showing up is a significant red flag.

The Financial Impact of a Bad Moving Experience

Let's put real numbers to it — because the math catches people off guard.

A furniture repair after a clumsy move: $150–$500 depending on the piece. A full replacement for something that can't be fixed: $300–$1,000. A missed workday because the crew ran four hours late and you couldn't leave until the job finished: another $200–$400 gone. One hotel night because the new apartment wasn't accessible yet: $120–$200.

Add that up and the $250 you saved on the cheapest quote is gone — and then some.

For long-distance moves, the stakes climb even higher. Delayed delivery, items in transit far longer than estimated, and damage claims that take months to resolve are the predictable outcomes of choosing long-distance moving services without verifying who's actually doing the work and whether they're carrying proper cargo insurance.

How Professional Movers Orlando Homeowners Trust Actually Reduce Risk

The difference between a smooth move and a disaster usually comes down to who's doing the work and what systems they operate under.

Professional residential movers in Orlando show up with the right crew size, the right equipment — moving blankets, floor runners, shrink wrap, proper dollies — and the experience to move a sectional around a tight stairwell without taking a chunk out of the wall.

More importantly, they put everything in writing. A licensed, insured moving company provides a binding estimate with itemized charges, a Bill of Lading on move day, and a clear claims process if something goes wrong. That paper trail is what gives you recourse — and exactly what unreliable movers avoid providing on purpose.

If you're moving a high-value home or need specialty handling, professional packing services add an additional layer of accountability — anything the crew packs, they're responsible for. That changes how carefully things get handled.

Questions to Ask Before Hiring a Moving Company in Orlando

Before you book anyone, get clear answers to these:

  • Are you licensed and insured?\
Ask for the FLDACS IM number and USDOT number. Verify both before committing.
  • Is this a binding estimate?\
Know exactly what's included and what could change the final price.
  • What additional fees might apply?\
Stairs, elevators, long carries, packing materials, fuel — ask specifically, in writing.
  • How do you handle damage claims?\
What's the process, the timeline, and what coverage actually applies?
  • Do you self-perform the move or broker it to subcontractors?For long-distance moves especially, this matters for accountability.

A company that answers all of these without hesitation has nothing to hide.

How to Find Reliable Movers in Orlando

It takes about 45 minutes and saves a lot of grief later.

Get at least three written quotes from moving companies in Orlando. Compare them line by line — not just the bottom-line number, but what's included. Verify each company's licensing through FDACS and FMCSA. Read reviews on Google and BBB looking for patterns, not outliers. Confirm your date in writing along with the crew size and truck type.

For apartment moves specifically — elevator reservations, COI requirements, loading zone coordination — work with movers who have direct experience navigating Orlando buildings. The apartment moving service page lays out exactly how Liberty Moves handles the building logistics that trip up less-experienced crews.

For senior moves and downsizing situations, the variables are different — timing, care level, coordination with family members who may not be present. The senior moving services page covers how those moves are planned and handled differently from a standard residential job.

Why the Cheapest Option Isn't Always the Most Affordable

There's a meaningful difference between a low price and a good value.

The most affordable movers Orlando residents actually recommend aren't the ones with the lowest hourly rate — they're the ones who showed up on time, handled the furniture carefully, came in close to estimate, and left the new apartment looking like it was supposed to. That's what value looks like in practice.

The cheapest quote gets you to move day. The right company gets you through it.

The Bottom Line

Choosing the wrong Orlando movers doesn't just create a bad day. It creates weeks of repairs, disputes, and out-of-pocket costs that dwarf whatever you thought you were saving on the front end.

The solution isn't complicated. Verify the license. Get everything in writing. Use the free moving cost calculator to benchmark what your move should actually cost before any conversation starts. Ask the uncomfortable questions. And if the price looks too good to be true, take ten minutes to find out why.

Your move deserves a crew that treats it like their reputation depends on it — because for a licensed, reputable moving company, it does.

Liberty Moves Orlando is fully licensed (USDOT 3455436, FLDACS IM3347), family-owned, and transparent on pricing from first quote to final invoice. Request a free quote at libertymovesorlando.com/contact-us or call (407) 641-2887.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the most common hidden fee from Orlando moving companies?\
Stair fees, long-carry charges (when the truck can't park close to the entrance), and fuel surcharges are the most frequently added costs that weren't in the original quote. Always ask for a complete list of potential additional charges — in writing — before signing anything.

How do I verify a moving company is licensed in Florida? \
Search the FDACS website (fdacs.gov) by company name or IM number for intrastate movers. For interstate moves, verify the USDOT number at safer.fmcsa.dot.gov. Both searches are free and take under two minutes.

What's the difference between released value and full value moving insurance?\
Released value protection — the default coverage included with every move — pays only 60 cents per pound per item. Full value protection covers actual repair or replacement at market value. For high-value or irreplaceable items, full value coverage is worth the additional cost.

Can I dispute a surprise charge from a moving company? \
Yes. Florida's Chapter 507 moving laws include consumer protections around estimates, final charges, and holdback practices for licensed movers. If a company holds your goods over a disputed amount, that's a violation you can report to FDACS.

Is Liberty Moves Orlando a broker or a direct carrier? \
Liberty Moves Orlando is a direct carrier. The same team that shows up to load your home completes the entire move — no subcontractors, no third-party handoffs, one point of contact from quote to delivery.

item.title: How to Choose the Best Moving Company in Orlando (Without Getting Burned by Hidden Fees)
item.description: Best moving companies Orlando locals trust share one thing: no hidden fees. Learn what to look for before you book — and what red flags to avoid.
item.author: Liberty Moves Orlando
item.content:encoded:
You've found a new place. You've set a date. And now you're staring at a list of "best moving companies in Orlando" on Google, wondering which one won't turn a $400 estimate into a $900 invoice by the time the last box hits the floor.

It happens more than it should. And in Florida — where moving scams are common enough that the state passed a dedicated anti-fraud law in 2024 — it's worth knowing exactly what to look for before you hand anyone your house keys and your couch.

This guide breaks down what separates a trustworthy Orlando mover from one that'll cost you.

Start With the License, Not the Reviews

Reviews matter. But a five-star rating doesn't tell you whether a company is legally allowed to move your belongings. In Florida, that's a separate question — and the answer is public.

Every intrastate mover (meaning any company moving you within Florida) must be registered with the Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services (FDACS) and hold an IM number. Interstate movers — those taking you across state lines — must also carry a USDOT number, which you can verify for free through the FMCSA's website.

If a company can't produce both numbers when you ask, stop the conversation there.

Liberty Moves Orlando carries USDOT 3455436 and FLDACS IM3347 — both verifiable in 30 seconds online. Any legitimate Orlando moving company should be able to say the same.

The Estimate Is Where Hidden Fees Are Born

The most common way Orlando movers burn customers isn't a bait-and-switch on the day of the move — it's a vague estimate on the front end.

Here's what to watch for:

Non-binding verbal quotes. If your estimate wasn't put in writing and signed by both parties, it's not an estimate — it's a conversation. Always get a written, itemized quote.

Hourly rates without caps or assumptions. "Starting at $X/hour" sounds affordable until the crew takes three hours longer than expected and you have no recourse. Ask what's included in the rate, how they handle delays, and whether there's a minimum.

"We'll quote the rest on-site" language. Some companies provide a low base rate, then add fuel surcharges, stair fees, long-carry fees, elevator fees, and packing material charges once they arrive and your furniture is already on the truck. At that point, you have very little leverage.

What to ask instead:

  • Is this estimate binding?
  • What additional charges could apply to my specific move?
  • Do you charge for stairs, long carries, or elevator waits?
  • Is there a fuel surcharge, and is it included in this quote?

A company offering transparent flat-rate pricing will answer all of these without hesitation. If a mover gets cagey, that hesitation is the answer.

Local vs. National: What "Best Long Distance Moving Company" Really Means for Orlando Movers

If you're searching for the best long distance moving companies from Orlando, the results will mostly surface national brands — large carriers like United Van Lines, Allied, or North American. These aren't bad options, but they come with a tradeoff most people don't expect: the crew that shows up at your door may be a subcontractor, not an employee of the company you hired.

That matters for a few reasons:

  • You don't always know who's handling your stuff
  • Accountability is diluted across multiple parties
  • Claims for damage can become a months-long back-and-forth between carrier and broker

A local long distance moving company that self-performs the move — meaning the same team that loads you also drives and delivers — gives you a cleaner chain of custody and a single point of contact if anything goes wrong.

For moves within Florida or to neighboring states, a locally-based Orlando mover with long-distance authority is often the sharper call.

What "Insured" Actually Means (Read This Before You Assume You're Covered)

Every legitimate moving company carries insurance. But what most customers don't realize is that released value protection — the default coverage included in every move — only pays out 60 cents per pound per item. Your 50-lb flat screen TV: $30. Your antique dresser: maybe $15.

That's not insurance. That's a liability floor.

If you have high-value items, ask specifically about full value protection — a separate, optional coverage that replaces or repairs items at current market value. This usually costs more, but it's the only version of moving insurance that actually functions like insurance.

Two things to confirm before booking any mover:

1. Their liability coverage type and limits

2. Whether third-party moving insurance is available through their quote process

Red Flags to Walk Away From

You don't need to be a moving industry expert to spot a problem. Most of the bad actors leave obvious signals:

  • No physical address or a P.O. Box as their business location
  • No license numbers available on the website or when asked directly
  • A quote dramatically lower than every other company you've spoken with
  • Demands a large cash deposit before the move date
  • Generic company name with no reviews older than 6 months (rebrand after negative review history is a common pattern in the scam-mover playbook)
  • No written contract offered before moving day

Florida's 2024 moving law (Chapter 507 updates) added new consumer protections around estimates, deposits, and holdbacks — but those protections only apply if you've hired a licensed carrier. The simplest protection is starting with a licensed, insured mover.

The Bottom Line

The best moving company in Orlando isn't always the cheapest one on the list or the most-advertised name. It's the one that shows you its license when you ask, puts the price in writing before the truck pulls up, and has a crew that actually knows how to move a house.

When you're comparing moving companies near me in Central Florida, Liberty Moves Orlando is licensed, insured, family-owned, and fully transparent on pricing. No surprise fees. No subcontractors. No runaround.

Get a free written quote at libertymovesorlando.com or call (407) 641-2887 — and ask us anything before you book.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q: How do I verify a moving company's license in Florida? \
A: Search the Florida Department of Agriculture and Consumer Services (FDACS) mover directory at fdacs.gov using the company name or IM number. For interstate movers, verify the USDOT number at safer.fmcsa.dot.gov. Both lookups are free and take under a minute.

Q: What's the difference between a binding and non-binding moving estimate? \
A: A binding estimate is a written, signed agreement that locks in the total price regardless of how long the move takes. A non-binding estimate is an approximation — the final bill can be higher. Always request a binding written estimate.

Q: Why are some Orlando moving quotes so much lower than others? \
A: Low quotes often exclude fees that appear on the final invoice: stair charges, elevator waits, fuel surcharges, and packing material costs. They may also reflect a broker quoting the job, not the company that will actually do the work. Ask exactly what's included — and get it in writing.

Q: What should I look for in a long distance moving company from Orlando? \
A: For interstate moves, confirm the company holds a valid USDOT number, verify their FMCSA safety record, ask whether they self-perform the move or broker it to subcontractors, and confirm full-value coverage is available. For moves to neighboring states (Georgia, North Carolina, Tennessee), a locally-based Orlando mover with long-distance authority is often the most accountable choice.

Q: Is Liberty Moves Orlando licensed for both local and long distance moves? \
A: Yes. Liberty Moves Orlando holds FLDACS IM3347 for intrastate moves and USDOT 3455436 for interstate moves. Both are verifiable through the official government databases linked above.

item.title: The Orlando Apartment Move Checklist: What to Do 30 Days Out (So Nothing Gets Left Behind)
item.description: Moving to a new Orlando apartment? This 30-day checklist keeps every step organized so nothing gets forgotten and moving day goes smoothly.
item.author: Liberty Moves Orlando
item.content:encoded:
You've signed the lease. The move-out date is circled on the calendar. And somewhere between juggling work, coordinating with your property manager, and figuring out where you put the bubble wrap — it suddenly hits you: there's a lot to do.

Apartment moving in Orlando has its own brand of chaos. You're not just packing boxes. You're navigating elevator reservation windows, dealing with loading zones that fill up by 9 a.m., coordinating HOA access for the new building, and doing all of it against a backdrop of Florida heat that peaks just when you're carrying a dresser to the third floor.

Starting 30 days out isn't overthinking it — it's the difference between a smooth move and a stressful one. This checklist breaks down exactly what to do, week by week, so nothing falls through the cracks and moving day feels manageable instead of miserable.

Why Apartment Moves in Orlando Require More Planning Than Most People Expect

If you've moved between single-family homes before, apartment moves will surprise you. The logistics are tighter, the building rules are stricter, and the margin for error on moving day is smaller.

Here's what makes Orlando apartment moves uniquely demanding:

1. Elevator reservations. Most mid- and high-rise buildings require you to reserve the service elevator in advance — sometimes two weeks out. Miss the window, and your crew is hauling furniture up stairs. Or worse, waiting.
2. Limited loading zones. Many apartment complexes have a single loading area shared by the entire building. Peak weekend slots go fast, and you may have a hard one-hour window.
3. Parking logistics. Depending on the complex, movers need a permit or a reserved spot close enough to the entrance to protect floors and keep the crew moving efficiently.
4. Gated community access. If you're moving into a gated property in Dr. Phillips, Baldwin Park, or Lake Nona, you'll need to arrange gate codes or escort access for the moving truck.
5. Narrow hallways and tight stairwells. Older buildings near College Park, Maitland, or Downtown Orlando weren't designed for modern sectional sofas. Knowing your measurements in advance isn't optional.
6. Orlando traffic. I-4 is not your friend on a Saturday morning. The shift between 17-92 and SR-436 can add 30–40 minutes to an arrival window if you haven't planned around peak times.
7. Weather. Summer in Orlando means afternoon thunderstorms, almost without exception. A morning start isn't just a preference — it's a practical strategy.

Planning 30 days out gives you the runway to solve all of these before moving day turns them into emergencies.

30 Days Before Your Move: Build the Foundation

This is the most important window. The decisions you make right now determine how controlled — or chaotic — everything else becomes.

Your 30-day checklist:

  • Confirm your official move-out date with your current landlord and move-in date with your new property manager
  • Give written notice per your lease terms (typically 30–60 days)
  • Contact apartment movers in Orlando to get quotes — good crews fill up fast on weekends, especially in May, August, and end-of-month dates
  • Book your moving company and get everything in writing: dates, crew size, pricing structure, cancellation policy
  • Start a moving folder — physical or digital — for your lease, mover confirmation, building contacts, and receipts
  • Begin a room-by-room declutter: anything you haven't used in a year should be donated, sold, or tossed
  • Order packing supplies: don't underestimate how many boxes you actually need

One thing worth doing early: get a realistic sense of your move cost. The Liberty Moves Orlando moving cost guide walks through what affects pricing for apartment moves in Central Florida — from floor level to crew size to distance — so there are no surprises on the invoice.

3 Weeks Before: Get Utilities Moving and Start Packing

The third week is about logistics infrastructure and starting the actual packing — beginning with the rooms you use the least.

  • Transfer electric (Duke Energy or OUC), water, and internet service to your new address with exact activation/deactivation dates
  • Update your mailing address through USPS — do this online at usps.com, then update banks, subscriptions, and your employer separately
  • Measure your furniture against the doorways, hallways, and elevator dimensions at your new apartment (this sounds tedious — it saves a lot of heartbreak on moving day)
  • Reserve your elevator. Contact the property management office at your new building now. Some buildings only hold weekend service elevator slots for 2–4 hours and book them out 2+ weeks in advance
  • Start packing: seasonal clothing, books, décor, items you won't need before the move
  • Organize important documents — lease agreements, insurance cards, medical records, passports — into a clearly labeled folder you will carry personally on moving day, not load onto the truck
  • If you have kids or pets, start making arrangements for who's watching them on moving day. Moving days are long and chaotic — neither kids nor dogs belong in the middle of it

2 Weeks Before: Lock In the Logistics

This window is about confirmation and continuation. You should be actively packing now, and all the building-level logistics should be confirmed.

  • Confirm your moving appointment — date, start time, crew size, truck size — with your local movers in Orlando
  • Reserve a loading zone or parking spot at both your current and new building — contact property managers directly and get confirmation in writing
  • Label every box clearly with its destination room and a basic contents description ("Master BR — bedding," not just "Bedroom")
  • Pack room by room; don't mix contents from different rooms in the same box
  • Set aside valuables — jewelry, cash, hard drives, sentimental items — to transport yourself
  • Schedule any donation pickups (Habitat for Humanity ReStores in Orlando do pickups; so does Goodwill for larger loads)
  • If you have fragile items like artwork, mirrors, or electronics, now is the time to prep them — or ask about professional packing services for the items you don't trust a cardboard box to protect

1 Week Before: The Final Prep Sprint

You should be living out of essentials at this point, with most of the apartment already packed. This week is for finishing and verifying.

  • Pack an "essentials bag" — think of it like an overnight bag: phone charger, toiletries, two days of clothes, medications, important documents, snacks, and your coffee setup. Load this into your car, not the truck.
  • Confirm your new building's specific move-in requirements: proof of renters insurance, deposit, parking tags, fob pickup, etc.
  • Defrost your refrigerator 24–48 hours before the move and clean it out completely
  • Double-check utility activation dates so you're not arriving at a dark, un-air-conditioned apartment in August
  • Dispose of anything that can't go on the truck: open paint cans, propane, cleaning chemicals, aerosols. Most hazardous materials are not covered under standard mover's liability.
  • Confirm your movers' arrival window — get a specific time, not just "morning"
  • Do a pre-move walkthrough of your current apartment and photograph everything for your security deposit documentation

Moving Day: What to Do from First Truck to Last Box

Even the best-planned moves have moving-day moments. Here's how to keep yours on track.

Before the truck leaves your current apartment:

  • Walk every room — every closet shelf, every bathroom cabinet, the balcony, under beds, inside the oven and dishwasher
  • Communicate clearly with your crew: tell them what's fragile, what gets special handling, and what room everything goes to at the destination
  • Protect floors and walls at both locations — any reputable residential moving company will bring floor runners and door frame padding, but confirm it
  • Keep your essentials bag with you at all times — not on the truck

When you arrive at the new apartment:

  • Inspect the unit before anything is unloaded: note any pre-existing damage and photograph it immediately. Report it to the property manager before the move-in is complete.
  • Test every utility — AC, hot water, stove, internet — before the crew leaves
  • Make sure the elevator reservation is active and communicate the window to your crew from the start

The Things People Almost Always Forget

Call this the "smack your forehead" list — the stuff that gets left behind or overlooked even by experienced movers:

  • Phone and device chargers — usually the last thing plugged in
  • Shower curtain and rings — already down, already "in the pile," never makes it to a box
  • Balcony furniture — out of sight during the main packing sweep
  • Contents of storage closets or outdoor storage units
  • The freezer — especially the stuff in the back
  • Spare keys and fobs — for your current unit, mailbox, gym, package locker
  • Mail forwarding — USPS takes 7–10 business days to process; don't wait until moving day
  • Cleaning supplies — you'll need them to clean the old unit out and you'll want them immediately at the new one

Why Professional Apartment Movers Make a Measurable Difference

This is where DIY moves quietly become expensive mistakes. The combination of tight hallways, elevator timing windows, parking logistics, and the sheer physicality of furniture-through-a-narrow-door situations is exactly where professional crews earn their fee.

Here's what you actually get with experienced apartment movers:

  • Speed. A two-person professional crew will out-pace four friends with a rented truck in almost every scenario — and they won't need lunch, a break every 30 minutes, or three trips to figure out how the truck ramp works.
  • Building experience. Liberty Moves Orlando moves apartments every week across the metro — Downtown Orlando, Baldwin Park, Thornton Park, Lake Nona, Altamonte Springs high-rises, gated communities in Dr. Phillips and Windermere. The crews know how to work within building rules, communicate with property managers, and keep the elevator window.
  • Furniture protection. Moving blankets, floor runners, shrink wrap, and proper box-truck padding aren't frills — they're what separates a scratch-free move from one that costs you the security deposit on your new place.
  • Liability coverage. If something breaks during a DIY move, that's on you. With a licensed, insured apartment moving service, you have actual protection.

Orlando-Specific Moving Tips Most Renters Skip

A few things worth knowing if this is your first Orlando apartment move:

  • Avoid I-4 between 7–9 a.m. and 4–7 p.m. The 408/I-4 interchange is consistently one of the worst bottlenecks in the region. A 9 a.m. move-out start that gets caught in the tail end of rush hour can push your schedule back an hour.
  • Start early in summer. In June, July, and August, temperatures in the truck and on the pavement start climbing by 10 a.m. A 7:30 or 8 a.m. start gets the heavy lifting done before it gets brutal.
  • Plan around afternoon storms. From roughly 2–5 p.m. during Florida summers, Central Florida sees convective thunderstorms with near-daily regularity. Scheduling your truck arrival to finish unloading before 1 p.m. is smart planning, not overcautious.
  • Check HOA moving windows. Some communities in Baldwin Park, Dr. Phillips, and Celebration have designated moving hours — typically 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. on weekdays only, or specific weekend windows. Check before you book.
  • Electronics need humidity-safe packing. Florida's heat and humidity are rough on unprotected electronics. Plastic bins with tight lids or sealed boxes beat standard cardboard for any move that runs during rainy season.

Conclusion: The Earlier You Start, the Easier It Gets

Apartment moves in Orlando aren't harder than they need to be — they're just unforgiving of last-minute planning. The elevator that isn't reserved, the loading zone that's taken, the box that never got labeled — these are all preventable, and they're all solved by starting earlier than feels necessary.

Thirty days is exactly the right amount of runway. Work through this checklist a week at a time, get your movers booked early, and give yourself the chance to arrive at your new apartment without the feeling that you've forgotten half your life.

When you're ready to book, Liberty Moves Orlando's apartment moving team is available across Central Florida — fully licensed (USDOT 3455436, FLDACS IM3347), insured, and familiar with the buildings, gates, and elevators in every corner of the metro. Request your free quote at libertymovesorlando.com or call (407) 641-2887 — peak weekend dates fill fast, especially in May and August.

Frequently Asked Questions

1. How far in advance should I book apartment movers in Orlando? \
For weekend and end-of-month moves, book at least 3–4 weeks out. May, August, and the last week of any month are the busiest windows — those dates can fill up even earlier. Weekday moves offer more flexibility and sometimes better pricing.
2. Do Orlando apartment buildings require movers to have a Certificate of Insurance (COI)?\
Many do, particularly newer or luxury properties. A COI names the building as an additional insured party and protects them if damage occurs during your move. Liberty Moves Orlando provides building-specific COIs at no charge — just send over your property manager's requirements and we'll get it to them before your move date.
3. How do I reserve a service elevator for my move?\
Contact your building's property management or leasing office directly. Most buildings require 1–2 weeks' notice and charge a small refundable deposit to hold the elevator. Your moving company can help coordinate the timing, but the reservation itself is typically made by the tenant.
4. What should I pack in my essentials bag for moving day?\
Pack anything you'll need in the first 24 hours regardless of what else gets unpacked: phone charger, medications, ID and insurance cards, a change of clothes, toiletries, laptop or tablet, snacks, and any important documents. Keep this bag in your car, not on the truck.
5. Can I move during Orlando's hurricane season?\
Yes — most moves happen without issue. That said, if a named storm is within the 5-day forecast cone, confirm your mover's rescheduling policy in writing before your move date. Liberty Moves Orlando works with customers individually on storm-related reschedules; just ask when you book.
6. How much does it cost to hire apartment movers in Orlando?\
Pricing depends on crew size, move duration, floor level, and distance. Local apartment moves in Orlando typically run 2–5 hours for a 1–2 bedroom. For a detailed breakdown of what goes into move pricing, see the Liberty Moves Orlando moving cost guide.

item.author: Liberty Moves Orlando
item.content:encoded:
